Introduction to Data Science Project

Author

Joe Harrison, Maddy Puryear

The main set of slides to reference are created by Justin Post here at NC State

Additionally, here is the bible for data science, and covers essentially all of the main Machine Learning methods.

More resources:

There are an overwhelming amount of resources. I link these only because I like them, and they provide an excellent jumping off point if you are interested.

Note that reading without actually implementing a project or summarizing what you have read is not going to be very helpful (even though it feels like it is).

  • https://r-graph-gallery.com/ <- The go-to place for data visualization
  • https://happygitwithr.com/index.html <- How does git work???
  • https://arrow-user2022.netlify.app/ <- How do I do stuff with large datasets?
  • https://github.com/adam-p/markdown-here/wiki/Markdown-Cheatsheet <- Just a quick reference guide on markdown

Below are some other books:

  • https://stat545.com/index.html <- Intro to R
  • https://r4ds.hadley.nz/ <- Intro to R
  • https://dtkaplan.github.io/DataComputingEbook/index.html#table-of-contents <- Intro to R
  • https://do4ds.com/chapters/intro.html <- DevOps for production code in data science
  • https://edwinth.github.io/ADSwR/index.html <- Agile philosophy

Project Goal:

By the end of this project, you should have a reproducable, visually appealing report.

Questions to address in your report include:

  • Are there any differences in the people who switch leagues? The people who switched leagues seem to have a higher rate of home runs over there career compared to those who didn’t. This could also be because of the smaller number of data points we have or people who switched leagues compared to those who didn’t.
new_league <- as.factor(data$NewLeague)
league <- as.factor(data$League)

switched_league <- which(new_league != league)
no_switch_league <- which(new_league == league)

career_homerun <- data$CHmRun
boxplot(career_homerun[switched_league])
boxplot(career_homerun[no_switch_league])
  • Are there specific hitters that over/under performed relative to their salary? Mike Schmidt under preformed. He was being payed a bit over $2k however he was at the bat 20 times over the league and only hit one time. Meanwhile Kirby Puckett overpreformed, only being payed around $300, he had one of the highest number of hits out of everyone and preformed decent in the other areas as well
library(dplyr)

money <- data$Salary
runs <- data$Runs

plot(money,runs)
data |> filter(Salary > 2000 & Runs < 20)

* Are players who spend longer in the league worth more? No because there is not a strong relationship between time spent in the league and salary earned.

years <- data$Years
AtBat <- data$AtBat

summary(years)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  1.000   4.000   6.000   7.444  11.000  24.000 
plot(years, money)
abline(lm(money ~ years))
plot(AtBat, money)
abline(lm(money ~ AtBat))
  • Please explain in a paragraph any potential ethical issues that might arise from the creation of this report.

If people where to use this report for hiring players for their team, the data alone shows who over preforms and under preforms. This neglects other soft skills like communication and teamwork. Someone who over preforms might be bad at working in a team and instead focuses on self-service, whereas someone who under preforms might be more team oriented, helping the members of their team. Focusing only on data and not taking into account un measurable things could lead to detriment towards your team, being less able to work with each other. Another issue that may arrive is using only people who have switched leagues, and not players that are currently in this league, because of the data found previously. This would lead in a disproportionate people within a league or tying to get into a league.

Stretch goals:

  • See if you can predict whether a player will be paid higher or lower than the median salary.
  • Are there any questions that come to your mind that you think are worthwhile to explore? Investigate! Explore!

Skills you will acquire along the way:

  • Understand the basics of manipulating data in R
  • Understand the basics of Statistical Inference
  • Gain experience grappling with randomness
  • Interpret multivariate regression output
  • Gain familiarity with common Machine Learning tools
  • Learn the basics of plotting within R

Start of project:

# 6/25/26
# clustering data :0
library(cluster)
Warning: package 'cluster' was built under R version 4.5.3
library(factoextra)
Warning: package 'factoextra' was built under R version 4.5.3
Loading required package: ggplot2

Welcome to factoextra!
Want to learn more? See two factoextra-related books at https://www.datanovia.com/en/product/practical-guide-to-principal-component-methods-in-r/
#hierarchy cluster
dist_df <- dist(na.omit(data$Salary))
hc <- hclust(dist_df)
plot(hc)
#K means

newData <- data[,c(2:14, 17, 18, 19)]
# Factored columns (and salary) does not work because of non numbers

set.seed(240)
kmeans.re <- kmeans(newData, centers = 3, nstart = 20)
kmeans.re

names <- data$rownames

names <- strsplit(names,' ')
names <- as.data.frame(names)
first_names <- names[1,]

first_names <- t(first_names)
tfn <- table(first_names)
# view(tfn)
hist(tfn)
plot(tfn)
pie(tfn)
barplot(tfn)
plot(tfn, type="l")
# Write Up (Things I noticed):
# The most Common name in this roster is Mike, belonging to 14 people
# Followed by Tony/Dave, belonging to 7 people
# The fist most common name belongs to double the people with the second most common
# BillyJo has a two name first name
# Bip is an interesting thing (So is Oddibe, Kirby, Candy)
# Time - 6, Steve - 6, Gary - 6, Bob - 6, Bill - 6
# The distance between 3rd and 2nd is less than the distance between 1st and 2nd
# I thought John would be the 1st most common name but I was wrong
# Most names showed up 1 - 2 times

\[\pi + 2\]

Download the data from here:

Alternatively, inspect element to find the web address. Search for the “Baseball Data”

# data <- read.csv("path/to/your/downloaded/file.csv")

#moved to report section
#Statistical tests

#find P-value
# testing difference in means (t-test)
switched_money <- data$Salary[switched_league]
no_switched_money <- data$Salary[no_switch_league]

t.test(switched_money, no_switched_money)

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  switched_money and no_switched_money
t = -0.22544, df = 21.858, p-value = 0.8237
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -195.7246  157.3581
sample estimates:
mean of x mean of y 
 518.0556  537.2388 
library(caTools)
Warning: package 'caTools' was built under R version 4.5.3
library(class)
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(1)

sample <- sample.split(data, SplitRatio = 0.7)
train <- subset(data, sample == TRUE)
test <- subset(data, sample == FALSE)

a <- lm(Salary ~ Years, data = train)
predicted_test_values <- coef(a)[1] + test$Years * coef(a)[2]
sum((test$Years - predicted_test_values)^2)/length(predicted_test_values) # SSE
[1] 342673.8
b <- lm(Salary ~ AtBat, data = train)
predicted_test_values_b <- coef(b)[1] + test$Years * coef(b)[2]
sum((test$Years - predicted_test_values_b)^2)/length(predicted_test_values_b) # SSE Lower SSE
[1] 18856.36

Functions that may be helpful in your analysis:

  • hclust()
  • pca()
  • lm(), glm(), glmnet()
  • kmeans()
  • t.test()
  • wilcox.test()

And here is just an example of the neat things that R can do:

# install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)

# https://simplemaps.com/data/world-cities
WorldCities <- read_csv("C:\\Users\\tmp00403\\Downloads\\simplemaps_worldcities_basicv1.91\\worldcities.csv")


BigCities <-
  WorldCities |>
  arrange(desc(population)) |>
  head(4000) |>
  select(lng, lat)

clusts2 <-
  BigCities |>
  kmeans(centers = 6) |>
  fitted("classes") |>
  as.character()

BigCities <-
  BigCities %>% mutate(cluster = clusts2)

BigCities |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lng, y = lat)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = cluster, shape = cluster)) +
  theme(legend.position = "top") + 
  theme_bw()
